original_reasoning: Both mean balsam (Commiphora), same substance. Clear Semitic-Greek loanword sharing. Near identical b-l-s-m.
Both sides are the same toponymic referent, essentially an identical place-name match.
Arabic Qustantiniyya = 'Constantinople'; Greek = 'of Constantine'. Direct etymological borrowing — the city named after the emperor.
The Arabic gloss explicitly gives a personal name, and Σαμουήλ is the same biblical name Samuel.
Both sides preserve a proper name or toponymic identity, so this is near-direct name continuity rather than drift.
Arabic الاحتلاط explicitly glossed as anger and vexation matches Greek angered almost identically.
Both sides are place names, and this looks like direct toponymic preservation rather than semantic drift.
Arabic البرذون: horse/pack animal. Greek βουρδών: mule. Direct borrowing of the same animal-category term; near-identical phonology. Classic Greco-Arabic loanword.
